text:
When I entered the narrow alley that went to the market all my senses were at once flooded with a cacophony of sights, sounds and smells that appeared to mix in one vivid patchwork of human activity. The atmosphere was filled with the smell of the aromatic blending of all sorts of exotic spices, grilled meat, and fresh produce, which again presented me with an odor experience I had never had.

The roadside was lined with the booths of vendors of every color with their goods overrunning their stands, composed of handwoven fabrics as well as wooden sculptures carved with fine attention to detail. Every merchant appeared to have his own way of working up a customer, in excited gestures, or in tuneful shouts in the local language, or in the way they had their most noticeable goods at the front of their stand.

The crowd was also an interesting diversity and purpose study. Local people walked about the market with a practiced skillfulness, deftly manoeuvring through the tight passages between stalls whilst negotiating prices in a way that was familiar and had their experience of several years in negotiating prices. Tourists, such as myself, in turn, moved at a slower pace, stopping often to explore something new or take some photos of specially photogenic objects.

However, what impressed me the most was that there was a certain atmosphere of genuine community that pervaded the whole market. This was not just a business area that was created to be used in commercial dealings but a social living breathing place where life schedules were kept and things were shared and cultural values were upheld by living it every day. I could see old women painfully choosing their vegetables and talking with great energy, children moving through the people with such skill, and artisans showing their work with a sense of pride.

The visual image was also impressive. Large heaps of brightly-colored fruits made natural pyramids which appeared to be against the laws of gravity. Merchants of cloth showed bolts of silk and cotton in all the possible shades, forming cascades of the rainbow that trapped and reflected the scattered rays of the sun that pierced through the improvised awnings over. Products were advertised in flowing script that I was unable to read and was added to the mystery and discovery.

Each time I explored the market further, I was slowly becoming accustomed to the pace of the market. The original sensual overload was replaced by the appreciation of the more subtle ecosystem that gave life to this wonderful place. Each communication, each purchase, each experience was a little fibre in a bigger picture of day to day life in this new world.

When I eventually and unwillingly approached the exit, with my arms loaded with purchases, both useful and frivolous, I found that this market had given me something, which was more important than souvenirs. It had given me a true understanding of what the core of a culture was and that would be what would stick in my memory long after the material possessions lost their appeal.

metadata.notes: English general writing about travel experience in a market - human written version
